patient_profile.relevant_prior_history: Mild cold 1 month before symptom onset, no new medications started in the 3 months prior to symptom onset

reported_symptom_details.onset: 3 weeks prior to consultation, initially mild
present_reported_symptoms: diurnal drooping eyelids (worse at end of day)
dysphagia limited to dry crunchy foods
reported_symptom_details.symptom_pattern: Symptoms worsen with activity and as the day progresses, improve significantly after periods of rest, nearly fully resolved after a full night of sleep

Neurologist (Neuromuscular Subspecialty).opinion: The patient's clinical presentation is highly consistent with generalized myasthenia gravis, a neuromuscular junction disorder, potentially triggered by the preceding mild viral illness. The constellation of fluctuating, activity-dependent weakness with diurnal variation (worsening over the day, improving with rest) involving extraocular, bulbar, and proximal limb musculature is classic for this condition in the absence of alternative explanatory findings.

supporting_evidence.content: 32-year-old female with no pre-existing autoimmune or neuromuscular conditions, history of mild cold 1 month prior to symptom onset, no new medications in the 3 months preceding symptom onset, no relevant family history of autoimmune or neuromuscular disease

3-week history of progressive symptoms including double vision, diurnal drooping eyelids, dysphagia limited to dry crunchy foods, upper limb weakness, and difficulty climbing stairs; symptoms worsen with activity and as the day progresses, improve significantly with rest and nearly fully resolve after a full night of sleep; no slurred speech reported

missing_evidence: Serum acetylcholine receptor (AChR) binding antibody assay results
Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) electrodiagnostic study results
Serum anti-muscle-specific kinase (MuSK) antibody assay results
Contrast-enhanced chest CT results to evaluate for thymoma/thymic hyperplasia
Ice pack test for ptosis results

Ophthalmologist.opinion: The patient's reported fluctuating diplopia and diurnal ptosis that improves with rest are classic ocular manifestations of myasthenia gravis, supporting the diagnosis of generalized myasthenia gravis proposed by the neuromuscular neurologist. No alternative ophthalmic etiology for the visual symptoms is suggested by the available clinical history.

supporting_evidence.content: 3-week history of double vision, diurnal drooping eyelids that worsen with activity and as the day progresses, improve significantly with rest and nearly fully resolve after a full night of sleep
Neuromuscular neurologist's assessment of generalized myasthenia gravis aligns with the classic ocular presentation of this condition, which is present in ~80% of myasthenia gravis patients at onset

missing_evidence: Ice pack test for ptosis results
Comprehensive ophthalmic examination (including margin reflex distance 1 measurement, extraocular motility assessment, cover test) results

results.measurement_name: Serum acetylcholine receptor (AChR) binding antibody assay
Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) electrodiagnostic study of facial and proximal limb muscles
Contrast-enhanced chest CT to evaluate for thymoma/thymic hyperplasia (associated with MG)

Comprehensive ophthalmic examination (including measurement of margin reflex distance 1 (MRD1) for each eye, extraocular motility assessment, and cover test to quantify and characterize the strabismus causing diplopia)

reasoning_chain.summary: 32-year-old female with no pre-existing autoimmune or neuromuscular conditions, no relevant family history of related conditions, history of mild cold 1 month prior to symptom onset, no new medications in the 3 months preceding symptom onset
evidence: Mild cold 1 month before symptom onset
No new medications started in the 3 months prior to symptom onset

reasoning_chain.summary: 3-week history of fluctuating weakness that worsens with activity and over the course of the day, improves significantly with rest, with involvement of extraocular muscles, bulbar function, and proximal limb musculature, no slurred speech reported
evidence: Symptom onset: 3 weeks prior to consultation, initially mild
Present symptoms: double vision, upper limb weakness, difficulty climbing stairs, diurnal drooping eyelids (worse at end of day), dysphagia limited to dry crunchy foods
Symptom pattern: worsen with activity and as the day progresses, improve significantly after rest, nearly fully resolved after a full night of sleep

reasoning_chain.summary: Neuromuscular Neurologist confirms clinical presentation fits classic generalized myasthenia gravis diagnostic criteria with no alternative explanatory findings
evidence: Neuromuscular Neurologist opinion: constellation of fluctuating, activity-dependent diurnal weakness involving extraocular, bulbar, and proximal limb domains plus preceding mild viral trigger is classic for myasthenia gravis

reasoning_chain.summary: Ophthalmologist confirms ocular symptoms are classic for myasthenia gravis, no alternative ophthalmic etiology identified, supports the diagnosis
evidence: Ophthalmologist opinion: fluctuating diplopia and diurnal ptosis improving with rest are classic ocular manifestations of myasthenia gravis, present in ~80% of MG patients at onset, no alternative ophthalmic cause for visual symptoms identified

final_report.final_reasoning: Consensus assessment from neuromuscular neurologist and ophthalmologist confirms the patient's clinical presentation of rest-responsive, diurnally fluctuating weakness affecting extraocular, bulbar, and proximal limb domains, preceded by a mild viral illness trigger, is highly consistent with generalized myasthenia gravis. Confidence is rated medium as confirmatory diagnostic tests (serum AChR/MuSK antibody assays, repetitive nerve stimulation study, chest CT, ice pack test, comprehensive ophthalmic exam) have not been completed to formally confirm the diagnosis and rule out associated findings like thymoma.
